_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: First-line treatment for intramedullary tumors is open surgical resection.
Surgery is indicated for all symptomatic lesions.
If a diagnosis of ependymoma is perceived, a complete surgical resection should be attempted.
If a diagnosis of astrocytoma is perceived, most clinicians advocate a more limited debulking of only the tissue that is clearly abnormal.

preauth.label: CT/MRI of other regions if symptomatic (optional)

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes include detailed history, signs & symptoms, evaluation findings, indication for procedure, and planned line of treatment?
History, physical examination, and radiological investigations confirm the diagnosis?
Clinical presentation and radiological investigations are indicative of surgery?

cpd_claim.q: Detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details provided?
Discharge summary with follow-up advise submitted?

common_queries: Clinical presentation or radiological investigations are not indicative of surgery.
Clinical notes are missing detailed history, signs & symptoms, evaluation findings, indication for procedure, or planned line of treatment.
History, physical examination, or radiological investigations did not confirm the diagnosis.
Missing CT/MRI Spine report at pre-authorization stage.
Post-operative CT Spine report not submitted at claim stage.
Histopathological/Biopsy report not submitted at claim stage.
Detailed Indoor case papers (ICPs) with daily vitals and treatment details not provided at claim stage.
Detailed procedure / operative notes not available at claim stage.

clinical_key_pointers: Spinal tumors occur with an incidence of 1.1 case per 100,000 persons. Intramedullary spinal tumors comprise approximately 2-4% of all Central Nervous System neoplasms.
The most common kinds of intramedullary tumors are ependymomas, cavernoma, astrocytomas, hemangioblastomas and miscellaneous.
In adults, ependymomas are the most common tumor type, accounting for 40-60% of all intramedullary spinal tumors, and the mean age of presentation being 35-40 years.
In children, astrocytomas are the most common tumor type, accounting for around 60% of all intramedullary spinal tumors, and the mean age of presentation is 5-10 years.
Symptoms often precede diagnosis by an average of 2 years for slow-growing tumors, while malignant or metastatic tumors present in weeks to months.
Pain and weakness are the most common presenting symptoms, with pain often being earliest and worse at night.
Progressive weakness may occur in arms or legs, often accompanied by impaired bowel, bladder, or sexual function and poor balance.
Rarely, symptoms of subarachnoid hemorrhage may be present.
Intra-tumoral hemorrhage can cause abrupt deterioration, most often associated with ependymomas.

Ependymomas are associated with: mean age at presentation of 43 years, slight female predominance, pain localized to spine (65%), pain worse at night or upon awakening, dysesthesia pain, long history of symptoms, Myxopapillary variant (mean age 21y, slight male predominance).
Astrocytomas are associated with: equal male and female prevalence, pain localized to spine, pain worse at night or upon awakening, paresthesia (abnormal sensation).
Hemangioblastomas are associated with: onset of symptoms by fourth decade (80% by age 40 years), familial disorder (von Hippel-Lindau syndrome) in a third of patients.

Physical examination sensory findings include: decreased touch, pain, and/or temperature sensation, hyperesthesia's, decreased proprioception, abnormal sensation below the level of lesion, abnormal sensation only at level of lesion (suspended level).
Physical examination hyperreflexia findings include: Hoffman sign for cervical lesions, clonus, extensor plantar response (Babinski sign).
Other physical examination findings include: motor weakness (late finding), spasticity, increased tone, muscle atrophy (late finding).
surgical_options: Limited debulking of clearly abnormal tissue (for astrocytoma)
additional_information.pre_op_preparation: Surgical fitness work-up including CBC, LFT, RFT, BT, CT, PT, INR, Serum Electrolytes, HIV, HBsAG, HCV etc. Radiological investigations: X-ray, CT scan for bone details, MRI scan with contrast, Bone scan. Metastasis workup: Chest X-ray, Mammography, Ultrasound of abdomen, Blood tests - PSA, CT/MRI of other regions if symptomatic.
additional_information.referral_criteria: Cases should be referred to the appropriate level of care (as required) for treatment under PMJAY.
